programs.name: Healthy Connections Medicaid
programs.what_it_does: Healthy Connections Medicaid provides medical coverage for hospital care, health center services, doctor visits, nursing homes, and long-term care services for eligible low-income seniors and disabled individuals. It covers seniors needing nursing home level care or community-based alternatives, including non-medical supports to help frail seniors remain at home.[1][2][3]
programs.who_its_for: Seniors aged 65+, blind or disabled individuals with low income and assets (e.g., single applicant: income under $2,982/month, assets under $2,000 in 2026) requiring nursing home level of care.[2][3]
programs.benefit_value: Full Medicaid-covered services including hospital, doctor, lab, X-ray, nursing home, and long-term care; income/asset limits vary by program and family size (e.g., 100% FPL: $1,330/month for one in 2026).[2][6]

programs.name: Community Choices Waiver
programs.what_it_does: This home and community-based services waiver provides assistance for elderly and disabled individuals requiring nursing home level care to live in their homes, adult foster care, or community residential care facilities. Benefits include home modifications, adult day health care, specialized medical equipment, and help with activities of daily living like bathing, mobility, and eating.[2]
programs.who_its_for: Elderly and disabled individuals needing nursing home level of care but preferring community living; must meet Medicaid financial and medical eligibility.[2]
programs.benefit_value: Non-medical supports such as home modifications, adult day health, equipment, and ADL assistance to avoid institutionalization.[2]

programs.name: South Carolina PACE
programs.what_it_does: Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly (PACE) offers comprehensive medical and social services to help frail seniors stay in the community. It integrates Medicare and Medicaid benefits through adult day health centers and in-home care.
programs.who_its_for: Medicare and Medicaid eligible seniors 55+ certified as needing nursing home level care but able to live safely in the community.
programs.benefit_value: Capitated comprehensive care including primary care, hospitalization, prescription drugs, social services, and transportation; value varies by enrollment (limited sites in SC).

programs.name: Medicare Savings Programs (QMB, SLMB, QI)
programs.what_it_does: These programs pay for Medicare costs including Part B premiums, deductibles, and co-payments based on income and assets. They help low-income Medicare beneficiaries afford coverage through Healthy Connections.[3]
programs.who_its_for: Medicare enrollees with income around $1,226/month (single) or $1,655 (couple); specific limits for QMB (100% FPL), SLMB (120% FPL), QI (135% FPL).[3][6]
programs.benefit_value: Covers Part B premium ($174.70/month in 2024+), deductibles, copays; amount depends on program and eligibility (e.g., full premium for QMB).[3]

programs.name: SNAP (Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program)
programs.what_it_does: SNAP provides monthly benefits via EBT card for purchasing nutritious food at approved stores. Administered statewide through SC Department of Social Services for low-income seniors.[3]
programs.who_its_for: Low-income seniors 60+ or disabled households meeting income limits (e.g., 130-200% FPL depending on deductions).
programs.benefit_value: Average $200-300/month per senior household; max benefits scale with household size (e.g., $291 single in 2026 base, adjusted annually).

programs.name: LIHEAP (Low-Income Home Energy Assistance Program)
programs.what_it_does: LIHEAP helps with heating and cooling bills, crisis assistance, and weather-related energy costs for low-income households. Delivered through SC community action agencies.[3]
programs.who_its_for: Low-income households including seniors at or below 150% FPL, priority for 60+, disabled, or those with high energy burdens.
programs.benefit_value: One-time grants $100-1,000+ per season for bills; crisis aid up to $1,200; varies by agency and funding.

programs.name: Weatherization Assistance Program (WAP)
programs.what_it_does: WAP funds energy efficiency upgrades like insulation, sealing, and furnace repairs to reduce energy costs and improve home safety. Administered via SC community action partnerships.
programs.who_its_for: Low-income households (200% FPL), priority for seniors 60+, disabled, families with children.
programs.benefit_value: Free home improvements valued $5,000-10,000 per household; lifetime energy savings of 20-30% on bills.

programs.name: I-Care (SHIP)
programs.what_it_does: The Insurance Counseling Assistance and Referrals for Elders (I-Care), South Carolina's SHIP, offers free one-on-one counseling on Medicare, Medicaid, and insurance options. It assists beneficiaries and caregivers with applications, plan choices, and appeals.[4][8]
programs.who_its_for: Medicare beneficiaries, families, and caregivers seeking health insurance guidance.
programs.benefit_value: Free counseling services; helps save thousands annually on premiums, drugs via optimal plan selection.

programs.name: SC Meals on Wheels
programs.what_it_does: Delivers nutritious meals to homebound seniors to promote health and independence. Coordinated through SC Department on Aging and local providers.
programs.who_its_for: Homebound seniors 60+ unable to prepare meals, often low-income or with mobility issues.
programs.benefit_value: 5-14 meals/week at $3-7 each (sliding scale or free for eligible); annual value $2,000-5,000.

programs.name: Respite Care Services
programs.what_it_does: Provides temporary relief for caregivers of seniors through in-home aides, adult day care, or facility stays. Offered via SC Department on Aging and waiver programs.
programs.who_its_for: Family caregivers of frail seniors 60+ or disabled adults at risk of placement.
programs.benefit_value: Subsidized hours/days (e.g., 20-40 hours/month); value $20-30/hour, up to $1,000+/month.

programs.name: SC Senior Community Service Employment Program (SCSEP)
programs.what_it_does: Offers part-time community service work training and employment for low-income seniors. Administered through SC Department on Aging partners.
programs.who_its_for: Unemployed low-income individuals 55+ (125% FPL), limited work experience.
programs.benefit_value: Wage at min. $7.25-15/hr, 20 hrs/week; annual income $7,000-15,000 plus employment placement.

programs.name: SC Legal Help for Seniors
programs.what_it_does: Provides free legal aid for income-eligible seniors on issues like housing, benefits, abuse, and wills. Delivered via SC Legal Services and Aging partners.
programs.who_its_for: Seniors 60+ at 125-200% FPL facing civil legal needs.
programs.benefit_value: Free representation/consults; saves $5,000+ per case in legal fees.

programs.name: Long-Term Care Ombudsman Program
programs.what_it_does: Advocates for residents in long-term care facilities, investigating complaints and ensuring quality care. Operated by SC Department on Aging.
programs.who_its_for: Residents of nursing homes, assisted living, and similar facilities in SC.
programs.benefit_value: Free advocacy resolving 80%+ complaints; protects rights and improves care conditions.

programs.name: Homestead Exemption
programs.what_it_does: The Homestead Exemption exempts the first $50,000 of a property's fair market value from local property taxes. It provides property tax relief to eligible homeowners.[4]
programs.who_its_for: Individuals who are 65 or older, totally disabled, or legally blind and have lived in South Carolina for at least one year.[4]
programs.whats_unique: This is a state-specific property tax exemption program funded and administered in South Carolina, offering a fixed $50,000 exemption not universally matched by other states' thresholds or eligibility.[4]
programs.benefit_value: Exemption on first $50,000 of property's fair market value from property taxes.[4]

programs.name: Elderly Simplified Application Project (ESAP)
programs.what_it_does: ESAP simplifies SNAP application by waiving the recertification interview, using data matches to reduce verification needs, and extending certification to 36 months. It makes nutrition assistance more accessible for seniors.[3]
programs.who_its_for: South Carolina residents 60 years or older whose household has no earned income and does not already receive SNAP.[3]
programs.whats_unique: State implementation of this federal SNAP variant with specific South Carolina-tailored simplifications unique to the state's administration.[3]
programs.benefit_value: Easier access to SNAP benefits with 36-month certification period.[3]

programs.name: Eldercare Trust Fund
programs.what_it_does: The Eldercare Trust Fund grants funding to local South Carolina nonprofits for senior programs like transportation, home modifications, and in-home mobility training. It supports innovative initiatives to help seniors age in place.[5]
programs.who_its_for: South Carolina nonprofit organizations with programs aligned to aging in place; indirectly benefits seniors through funded services.[5]
programs.whats_unique: State-funded trust specifically granting to SC nonprofits for unique local eldercare programs not available as a standard federal offering.[5]
programs.benefit_value: Grants to nonprofits for senior services such as home mods and transport (amounts vary).[5]
